point-12.title: A disagreement is a review prompt, not a verdict
point-12.lesson: The useful question is why LuckyJ and NAGA split: safety, route count, value, pressure, or a real mistake.
point-12.prompt: Bucket the disagreement before judging it: blunder, strategic trade-off, or table-reading idea.
point-12.answer: Do not chase match rate. Chase explainable decisions with fewer unpriced risks.

point-02.title: Keep the branch point alive
point-02.lesson: LuckyJ often keeps tiles that still serve yaku, value, or safety, even when that makes the current shanten number look worse.
point-02.prompt: List the live routes before cutting: closed riichi, open yaku, value upgrade, chiitoi, and fold.
point-02.answer: Do not collapse into the cleanest single route until the table has forced that commitment.

point-05.title: Cut the future liability now
point-05.lesson: The floater is bad because a slow hand may be forced to discard it after the table becomes dangerous.
point-05.prompt: Find the tile you will hate discarding after the first riichi or second call.
point-05.answer: If the hand is far away and the tile has no value job, remove it before it becomes expensive.

point-09.title: Reprice the hand after every draw
point-09.lesson: A hand that was pushing one turn ago can become a fold when the next required discard is bad. The chain of future discards matters more than the current tile alone.
point-09.prompt: Count the current discard plus the next two likely discards before calling the hand a push.
point-09.answer: If the path contains several live danger tiles, downgrade the hand even when it is close.

point-14.title: Keep river-safe and suji exits on purpose
point-14.lesson: LuckyJ often keeps a tile because it is already genbutsu or suji against an opponent river, especially before spending safer exits would make the next threat impossible to answer.
point-14.prompt: Before discarding a flexible tile, count which tiles in your hand are genbutsu or suji to each opponent and which of them are still useful next turn.
point-14.answer: Keep the safe tile while it buys future choice; spend it only when the hand has become worth the risk or the draw-point/fold route is already decided.

point-10.title: Late precision beats late invention
point-10.lesson: Late choices should have exact upside: winning tenpai, safe tenpai, or clean fold. Speculative value routes have mostly expired.
point-10.prompt: In the third row, state whether you are winning, taking safe tenpai, or folding.
point-10.answer: The later the hand, the more the example is a counting drill rather than a style imitation.

point-03.title: Bad closed shapes buy calls
point-03.lesson: The call is valuable when the closed route is mostly imaginary and the open hand creates yaku, tempo, or real tenpai pressure.
point-03.prompt: Before calling, say what the call creates: yaku, tenpai, real 1-shanten, denial, or safe draw equity.
point-03.answer: Call poor closed shapes when the exposed hand has a contract and still leaves a way to stop.

players.hand: 2m 2m 1s 3s 4s 6s E S S W N P P

point-03.hand: 2m 2m 1s 3s 4s 6s E S S W N P P

point-04.title: Open hands must keep an exit
point-04.lesson: After opening, the important tile is often the safe tile left behind, not the tile called. LuckyJ's open hands work because they are not all-in by accident.
point-04.prompt: After the call, identify the next safe discard before admiring the new shanten.
point-04.answer: A cheap exposed hand with no exit is usually not LuckyJ-style aggression; it is being forced to push.

players.hand: 1s 3s 4s 6s E S S W N N

point-04.hand: 1s 3s 4s 6s E S S W N N

point-07.title: Call for tempo with a contract
point-07.lesson: The call has a job beyond completing blocks: speed up a poor hand, contest the table, break a bonus window, or preserve draw equity.
point-07.prompt: No contract, no call. Name the contract before exposing the hand.
point-07.answer: A good tempo call changes the round's clock without making you helpless on the next threat.

point-06.title: Build the hand class early
point-06.lesson: A cheap bad-shape future is not worth optimizing toward when the score job needs more. Early acceptance is the cheapest thing to spend.
point-06.prompt: Ask what score class this hand needs before preserving maximum ukeire.
point-06.answer: Keep dora, yaku, and shape upgrades when the fast hand would be too small or too brittle.

point-08.title: Riichi converts pressure into equity
point-08.lesson: LuckyJ does not wait for a perfect shape when riichi itself changes the table. The declaration can force rivals to fold, deny calls, or lock in placement value.
point-08.prompt: Before riichi, ask who must now respond to you and whether better upgrades are realistic.
point-08.answer: If the wait is serviceable and the table must respect the stick, declaration pressure can be worth more than waiting.

point-13.title: Clean yakuhai against unproven open hands
point-13.lesson: When another player calls without showing a clear yaku, singleton dragons and live value winds can become their missing contract. LuckyJ often removes those tiles before they turn into the price of continuing.
point-13.prompt: After an opponent opens, ask which yakuhai still lets that hand win.
point-13.answer: If your hand is not ready to punish them and the yakuhai has no strong job, clean it before the open hand gets to use it.

point-11.title: Drawn-hand points are attack equity
point-11.lesson: Late defense is not always surrender. Safe tenpai or harmless 1-shanten can win the hand without winning the hand.
point-11.prompt: When the hand is unlikely to win, count whether a safe route to tenpai still exists.
point-11.answer: Choose the safe discard that leaves the most realistic tenpai path for the draw.

point-01.title: Placement changes the price of the same tile
point-01.lesson: The discard is not judged in a vacuum. A leading or fragile placement can make future defense more valuable than a prettier immediate shape.
point-01.prompt: Before choosing, name the hand's job: win now, protect placement, escape last, or keep future exits.
point-01.answer: Copy the risk budget, not the tile. The correct discard is the one that serves the placement job while leaving a playable next turn.
